records.ety: [F. consentir, fr. L. consentire, -sensum, to feel together, agree; con- + sentire to feel. See Sense.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Consented; p. pr. & vb. n. Consenting.]

defs: To agree in opinion or sentiment; to be of the same mind; to accord; to concur.
quotes: And Saul was consenting unto his death.
Flourishing many years before Wyclif, and much consenting with him in jugdment.

defs: To indicate or express a willingness; to yield to guidance, persuasion, or necessity; to give assent or approval; to comply.
quotes: My poverty, but not my will, consents.
And whispering “I will ne'er consent,” -- consented.

defs: To grant; to allow; to assent to; to admit.
quotes: Interpreters . . . will not consent it to be a true story.

defs: Agreement in opinion or sentiment; the being of one mind; accord.
quotes: All with one consent began to make excuse.
They fell together all, as by consent.

defs: Correspondence in parts, qualities, or operations; agreement; harmony; coherence.
quotes: The melodious consent of the birds.
Such is the world's great harmony that springs From union, order, full consent of things.

defs: Voluntary accordance with, or concurrence in, what is done or proposed by another; acquiescence; compliance; approval; permission.
quotes: Thou wert possessed of David's throne By free consent of all.

defs: Capable, deliberate, and voluntary assent or agreement to, or concurrence in, some act or purpose, implying physical and mental power and free action.
